content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 23, 2010

Thomas A. Willis
Remcho, Johansen & Purcell
201 Delores Avenue
San Leandro, CA 94577

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-146

Dear Mr. Willis:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign finance provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Does Section 85320 prohibit contributions, expenditures, or independent expenditures in connection with a ballot measure by a trust established under the laws of the State of California if the trust is settled by a foreign trust?

CONCLUSION

Under the facts you have provided, the foreign trust is the source of any contribution, expenditure, or independent expenditure made by a second trust it has established under the laws of the State of California. Thus, any contribution, expenditure, or independent expenditure in connection with the qualification or support of, or opposition to, any state or local ballot measure by the trust established under the laws of the State of California is prohibited under Section 85320 as a contribution or expenditure by a foreign principal made through another person.

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114, Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-10-146
Page No. 2

FACTS

You seek assistance on behalf of a foreign trust that intends to settle a second trust under the laws of the State of California (the "U.S. Trust"). The foreign trust, which you represent, is administered by foreign trustees. The foreign trust is an irrevocable discretionary trust and has been designated to make donations to charitable organizations, including those described by Sections 501(c)(3) and 501(c)(4) of the Internal Revenue Code.

As anticipated, the U.S. Trust will be irrevocably settled by the foreign trust and will be categorized as a "United States trust" for federal income tax purposes. Thus, the U.S. Trust will fulfill the requirements set forth in Section 7701(a)(30)(E) of the Internal Revenue Code. As you have presented the relevant portions of Section 7701(a)(30)(E) of the Internal Revenue Code, a trust will be a "United States person" under the code if a court within the United States is able to exercise primary supervision over the administration of the trust and one or more United States persons have the authority to control all "substantial decisions" of the trust. You state that the Internal Revenue Code defines "substantial decisions" to include decisions as to (1) whether and when to distribute income and corpus; (2) the amount of any distribution; (3) the selection of a beneficiary and principal; (4) whether a receipt is allocable to income or corpus; (5) whether to terminate the trust; (6) whether to compromise, arbitrate, or abandon claims of the trust; (7) whether to sue on behalf of the trust or to defend suits against the trust; and (8) whether to remove, add, or replace a trustee. (See C.F.R. Section 301.7701-7(d)(1)(ii).) The U.S. Trust will comply with all federal and state reporting requirements applicable to United States trusts.

Furthermore, the U.S. Trust will not conduct any business for profit. The trustee's primary responsibility will be the preservation of trust property, and the trustee will not be empowered to operate a business or business. The trustee of the U.S. Trust will be either (1) an individual that is a citizen or permanent resident of the United States or (2) a trust company organized and operating under the laws of the United States or of any state or other place subject to the jurisdiction of the United States. The U.S. Trust will have at least one fiduciary, which will also carry on all of its duties within the United States.

The only beneficiaries of the U.S. Trust will be charitable organizations described in Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code and non-profit civic leagues and organizations described in Section 501(c)(4) of the code. As part of its efforts, the U.S. Trust would also like to support ballot measures in the State of California to the extent that the measures are compatible with its goals of promoting long-term governmental reform. The U.S. Trust will not make any contributions to or expenditures for or against any federal, state, or local candidates.

An advisory panel composed solely of U.S. citizens will make recommendations to the trustee of the U.S. Trust with regard to individual organizations to which the U.S. Trust may make distributions. The trustee will have full discretion to accept or reject these recommendations in selecting beneficiaries. No person serving on the panel will be a trustee of the foreign trust or a named beneficiary of the foreign trust.

File No. I-10-146
Page No. 3


ANALYSIS

In pertinent part, Section 85320 provides the following:

"(a) No foreign government or foreign principal shall make, directly or
through any other person, any contribution, expenditure, or independent
expenditure in connection with the qualification or support of, or opposition to,
any state or local ballot measure.

[‖]

"(c) For the purposes of this section, a "foreign principal" includes the
following:

"(1) A foreign political party.

"(2) A person outside the United States, unless either of the following is
established:

"(A) The person is an individual and a citizen of the United States.

"(B) The person is not an individual and is organized under or created by
the laws of the United States or of any state or other place subject to the
jurisdiction of the United States and has its principal place of business within the
United States.

"(3) A partnership, association, corporation, organization, or other
combination of persons organized under the laws of or having its principal place
of business in a foreign country.

"(4) A domestic subsidiary of a foreign corporation if the decision to
contribute or expend funds is made by an officer, director, or management
employee of the foreign corporation who is neither a citizen of the United States
nor a lawfully admitted permanent resident of the United States."

Based upon the facts you have provided, it is clear that the foreign trust is a foreign
principal pursuant to Section 85320(c)(3). However, as provided by Section 85320(c)(2)(B), it
appears that the U.S. trust established under the laws of the State of California is not a foreign
principal. Notwithstanding the classification of either trust in question, Section 85320(a)
prohibits a foreign principal from making any contribution, expenditure, or independent
expenditure in connection with the qualification or support of, or opposition to, any state or local
ballot measure either directly or through another person. Thus, the critical determination is
whether the foreign trust is making a contribution, expenditure, or independent expenditure
through the U.S. Trust under the circumstances you have presented.

File No. I-10-146
Page No. 4


In many circumstances, determining whether a foreign principal is the source of a
contribution, expenditure, or independent expenditure made through a person within the United
States may be complicated because funds may be intermingled prior to the contribution,
expenditure, or independent expenditure. In these cases, to determine whether the source of the
funds is the foreign principal, it is often necessary to determine whether or not the foreign
principal made the decision to contribute or expend funds. For example, Section 85320(c)(4)
expressly prohibits contributions, expenditures, or independent expenditures by a domestic
corporation in those cases in which "the decision to contribute or expend funds is made by an
officer, director, or management employee of the foreign corporation who is neither a citizen of
the United States nor a lawfully admitted permanent resident of the United States." Conversely,
nothing within the Act permits a contribution, expenditure, or independent expenditure of funds
merely because the decision to contribute or expend funds is made by a person who does not
qualify as a foreign principal when it is clear that the funds were provided by a foreign principal.

From the facts you have presented, it appears that every effort will be made in
establishing the U.S. Trust to ensure that the ultimate decision to contribute or expend funds will
be made by a person other than a foreign principal. However, the structure of the U.S. Trust
does not negate the facts that the U.S. Trust will consist of funds primarily, if not solely, derived
from the foreign trust and that the foreign trust maintains a level of control over the U.S. Trust in
its ability to select the U.S. Trust's trustee and advisory panel and by earmarking the use of trust
funds to those purposes supported by the foreign trust.

Under these circumstances, we find no compeling reason to look beyond the foreign trust
in determining the source of a contribution or expenditure of funds made by the U.S. Trust
because it is unquestionable that the vast majority, if not all, of the funds available to the U.S.
Trust will be provided by the foreign trust. Thus, any contribution, expenditure, or independent
expenditure made by the U.S. Trust in connection with the qualification or support of, or in
opposition to, any state or local ballot measure is prohibited under Section 85320 as a
contribution or expenditure by a foreign principal made through another person.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl
